title: The 850 Lines of Code Between Your Users and a Double-Booking Disaster
introduction: A routine pull request on an open-source calendar app revealed a terrifying reality: your booking system might be broken and you do not even know it. When two users clicked 'confirm' at the same millisecond on Cal.com, an open-source Calendly alternative, both got a success message — and both walked away with a confirmed booking [1]. The problem? 850 lines of code separated the availability check from the actual booking insert. Nothing was holding the door.

sections.heading: Hook — The Click That Broke the Rules
sections.content: Imagine this: you are the engineer on call when your CEO forwards a support ticket. A user booked a meeting slot. So did someone else. Same time, same host. Two confirmations, one slot, zero explanation. You check the logs. Both transactions passed the availability check. Both inserted a booking. And both returned 200 OK to the user. Sound familiar? If you have ever built a reservation system — whether for meetings, hotel rooms, or concert tickets — you have probably stared at this exact bug. It is not a schema problem. It is not a hardware failure. It is a race condition hiding in plain sight, and it lives in the gap between checking availability and committing the booking.

sections.heading: Problem — The Infinite Gap Between Check and Commit
sections.content: Every booking system follows the same basic dance: check if a slot is available, then claim it. The problem is the distance between those two steps. In a single-threaded world, that distance is zero — no one else cuts in line. But the real world is not single-threaded. Your API server runs dozens of worker processes. Your database handles hundreds of concurrent connections. And in that microscopic window between SELECT and INSERT, a second request can sneak through. The SELECT says the slot is free. The second SELECT also says the slot is free. Both INSERT. Now you have a double booking. This is the classic lost update problem, and it is the most common concurrency bug in database-backed applications. Many developers reach for application-level mutexes or distributed locks, but those introduce their own failure modes: what happens when the lock holder crashes? What about network partitions? The database already has the tools to solve this — you just need to know how to use them.

sections.heading: Real-World Case — Cal.com
sections.content: Cal.com is an open-source Calendly alternative with thousands of self-hosted instances. In early 2024, a developer noticed something unsettling in the booking endpoint: the availability check happened in one part of the codebase, and the actual booking creation happened 850 lines later — with no lock held in between [1]. During peak load, two concurrent requests could both read the same availability as 'free' and both proceed to insert bookings. The fix was remarkably elegant and needed zero schema changes. The team already had a composite index on (userId, status, startTime). By wrapping the availability check and booking insert in a single SERIALIZABLE transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE, they turned a silent double-booking into a clean [200, 409] response pattern. One succeeds, the other gets a precise 'booking conflict' error. No phantom bookings. No unhappy customers. The PR that fixed it [1] is a masterclass in surgical database fixes — minimal code changes, maximum correctness impact.

sections.heading: Deep Dive — SERIALIZABLE Isolation and the Weapons PostgreSQL Gives You
sections.content: PostgreSQL offers four isolation levels, but only one eliminates all concurrency anomalies: SERIALIZABLE [2]. At this level, the database guarantees that concurrent transactions produce the same result as if they ran one after another. If a conflict is detected, one transaction is aborted with error 40001 — and your application handles the retry. Here is the key insight many engineers miss: READ COMMITTED (the default in PostgreSQL) is not enough for booking systems. Under READ COMMITTED, two concurrent transactions can both read the same row, see it as available, and both proceed to insert. SELECT FOR UPDATE changes the game — it locks the rows it reads, so the second transaction blocks until the first commits [6]. But even FOR UPDATE has a subtlety: it only protects the rows you explicitly lock. If your availability check reads from a summary table and your booking writes to a separate calendar table, you can still get inconsistent states. The fix is to lock the authoritative rows — the date-specific slots — within the same transaction that performs the insert. This is where MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) becomes your friend rather than your enemy [2]. PostgreSQL's MVCC allows snapshot reads without blocking, so your availability queries for non-booking purposes remain fast. Only the actual booking transaction pays the locking tax. This is the best of both worlds: high read throughput and write safety.

sections.heading: Workflow — Anatomy of a Safe Booking Transaction
sections.content:
The following Mermaid sequence diagram traces two concurrent booking requests through a properly locked system. Notice how the second request does not silently succeed — it hits the lock, retries, discovers the slot is taken, and returns a clean 409.

A booking request enters the API layer, which first checks a cache for quick rejection of obviously taken slots. The authoritative check happens inside a PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE transaction. The BEGIN is issued. SELECT ... FOR UPDATE locks the specific date range for this property. If another transaction holds this lock, the second request waits — it does not silently insert. Once the lock is acquired, the availability is re-checked against the authoritative database. If still available, the booking INSERT runs and the transaction commits. The second request, upon retry, re-reads with FOR UPDATE, discovers no available slots, and returns 409 Conflict. The cache is invalidated on the first commit, so subsequent reads also reflect the new state.

sections.heading: Code Example — The Booking Transaction Pattern in PostgreSQL
sections.content: Here is the production pattern that prevents double bookings. The critical detail is that the availability check and the booking insert share the same transaction and the same row-level lock.

sections.heading: Lessons Learned — What Every Developer Should Take From This
sections.content: First, the distance between a check and an action is where bugs live. If your codebase has more than a handful of lines between reading a resource's state and writing to it, that gap is a ticking bomb. Second, your database is more powerful than you think. Many teams reach for Redis locks, ZooKeeper, or custom distributed coordination before fully utilizing what PostgreSQL provides out of the box. SELECT FOR UPDATE and SERIALIZABLE isolation are mature, battle-tested features [3]. Third, schema changes are not always the answer. Cal.com's fix required zero schema changes — just a different transaction boundary around existing queries and indexes [1]. Finally, measure your lock contention. A booking system for a small SaaS might never hit lock conflicts. But if you are building the next Airbnb or Cal.com, hot properties will cause contention. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff, monitor lock wait times, and consider circuit breakers for properties that see extreme concurrent demand [7].

realWorldCase.incident: Race condition in booking endpoint where two concurrent requests selecting the same time slot could both pass the availability check and create duplicate bookings — there was an ~850-line gap between the availability check and the booking insert where no lock was held.
realWorldCase.impact: Two concurrent booking requests now produce [200, 409] — one succeeds, the other gets a clean 'booking conflict error' instead of silently creating a double-booking. The fix required no schema changes.

diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant ClientA
    participant ClientB
    participant API
    participant Cache
    participant DB as PostgreSQL

    ClientA->>API: POST /bookings
    ClientB->>API: POST /bookings (simultaneous)

    API->>Cache: Quick availability check
    Cache-->>API: Available (could be stale)

    API->>DB: BEGIN SERIALIZABLE
    API->>DB: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE
    Note over DB: Row-level lock on property date range

    API->>DB: Verify availability
    DB-->>API: Available (authoritative)

    API->>DB: INSERT INTO bookings
    API->>DB: COMMIT
    DB-->>API: Booking confirmed
    API-->>ClientA: 200 OK

    Note over ClientB: Second request arrives during lock
    API->>DB: BEGIN SERIALIZABLE
    API->>DB: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE
    Note over DB: Lock conflict — waits, then retries

    API->>DB: Verify availability
    DB-->>API: Slot already taken
    API->>DB: ROLLBACK
    API-->>ClientB: 409 Conflict
diagramLabel: Concurrent Booking Flow with PostgreSQL Row-Level Locking

images.alt: Rows of server racks in a brightly lit data center, representing the infrastructure behind booking systems
images.caption: Every booking request hits your database infrastructure — if your transactions are not properly isolated, contention turns into corrupted data.

images.alt: Computer screen displaying lines of code, representing the debugging process for race conditions
images.caption: Fixing concurrency bugs often requires zero schema changes — just smarter transaction boundaries, as Cal.com demonstrated.

codeExample.explanation: The function opens a SERIALIZABLE transaction — the only isolation level that prevents all three concurrency anomalies (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads). It then uses SELECT FOR UPDATE to acquire row-level locks on the matching availability rows. If no row is found (the slot is taken), the transaction rolls back immediately with a 409 error. If the slot is available, the booking is inserted and the transaction commits. If a serialization failure occurs (PostgreSQL error code 40001), the function retries with exponential backoff — 50ms, 100ms, then 200ms — with jitter to avoid thundering herd problems. The client connection is released in the finally block to prevent connection leaks.

conclusion: The gap between checking a resource's state and changing it is where concurrency bugs breed. Cal.com learned this the hard way — 850 lines of vulnerable code between SELECT and INSERT. But the fix was not a new database, a distributed lock manager, or a complete rewrite. It was a smarter transaction boundary using tools PostgreSQL already provided: SERIALIZABLE isolation, SELECT FOR UPDATE, and a composite index that was already there. The next time you write a check-then-act sequence — whether it is booking a meeting, reserving inventory, or assigning a username — ask yourself: what happens if two requests arrive at the same time? If the answer is not 'one succeeds, the other gets a clean error,' then you have a race condition. Your database already has the tools to fix it.
metaDescription: How a 850-line gap between availability checks and booking inserts caused double-bookings at Cal.com, and how PostgreSQL's SERIALIZABLE isolation with SELECT FOR UPDATE fixed it.

socialSnippet.hook: Ever wondered what happens when two people book the same slot at the exact same millisecond? 🕐
socialSnippet.body:
• Cal.com found 850 lines of code between their availability check and booking insert — zero locks held
• Two concurrent requests both got 200 OK for the same time slot. Both users got a confirmed booking.
• The fix? Zero schema changes. Just smarter transactions using PostgreSQL's SELECT FOR UPDATE
• One requests gets 200. The other gets 409. No more phantom double-bookings.
socialSnippet.cta: Full architecture breakdown with code example and Mermaid diagram in the article 👆
